subject: Chemistry
title: Kanada: India's Ancient Atomist
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Kanada: India's Ancient Atomist.

hook.text: Around 600 BC—before Democritus—the Indian philosopher Kanada proposed that all matter is made of 'paramanu.'

buildup.text: Kanada taught that paramanu are eternal, indestructible, and combine in pairs and triplets to form visible matter.

discovery.text: He classified matter into nine types: earth, water, fire, air, space, time, direction, mind, and soul.

twist.text: His Vaisheshika school described atoms combining through inherent properties—an early concept of chemical bonding.

climax.text: Kanada's ideas developed independently from Greek atomism. Two civilizations reached the same insight separately.

punchline.text: Two continents, same idea: matter has smallest parts. Convergent thinking.

quiz.question: What is significant about Kanada's atomic theory?
options: It developed independently from Greek atomism, showing convergent thinking
It was mathematically more precise than Democritus's version
It correctly predicted the number of elements
